history: Tabular dataset (UCI Heart Disease / Kaggle House Prices) → Scikit-learn supervised learning pipeline (EDA, feature engineering, baseline vs ensemble, hyperparameter tuning)
description: The student will build a predictive pipeline using a public tabular dataset to perform binary classification or regression. The core mechanism involves exploratory data analysis, handling missing values, and comparing a linear baseline against an ensemble method. The two scenarios compare model performance before and after hyperparameter tuning, highlighting how specific feature transformations impact final evaluation metrics.
consultant_notes: Standard approach, well-scoped for 14 days with public datasets and scikit-learn.
annotator_notes: Very feasible with standard tools and public datasets, but the scope may be a bit broad if the student tries to include EDA, feature engineering, two models, hyperparameter tuning, and full error analysis within 14 days. These are doable using scikit-learn, I think learning of PyTorch is necessary now so the assignment/project should reflect that.

history: Text-based dataset (IMDb reviews / Twitter Sentiment) → NLP with NLTK or Spacy and Scikit-learn (TF-IDF/Word2Vec, Naive Bayes vs RNN)
description: The student will build a sentiment analysis or topic classification model using a dataset of text entries. The core mechanism involves text preprocessing and converting text to numerical vectors using TF-IDF or Word2Vec. The two scenarios compare a Bag of Words approach with Naive Bayes against a more modern approach using a pre-trained embedding or simple RNN, focusing on confusion matrices to identify misclassified classes.
consultant_notes: NLP pipeline is well-defined; RNN adds appropriate depth for a 14-day project.
annotator_notes: This is feasible with common NLP datasets and Scikit-learn baselines, but the scope becomes large for 14 days if the student must cover preprocessing, TF-IDF/Word2Vec, Naive Bayes, embeddings or an RNN, and error analysis; it would fit better if the neural model is kept simple, preferably implemented in PyTorch.

history: Image dataset (MNIST / CIFAR-10 / Kaggle Plant Pathology) → Computer Vision with PyTorch or TensorFlow/Keras (custom CNN vs transfer learning with ResNet/MobileNet)
description: This project uses deep learning to perform image classification. The core approach involves building and training a CNN from scratch, then comparing against transfer learning by fine-tuning a pre-trained model. The deliverable includes visualizations of loss/accuracy curves and saliency maps or incorrectly classified examples.
consultant_notes: PyTorch + transfer learning is strong; scope manageable if dataset stays simple (MNIST/CIFAR-10).
annotator_notes: This is a strong fit because it uses PyTorch/TensorFlow and includes two distinct architectures; however, the scope should stay with MNIST or CIFAR-10 rather than a complex Kaggle dataset to remain manageable in 14 days. 3/5 for scope because there is not much data cleaning involved. The project should cover multiple aspects of machine learning.

history: Unlabeled dataset (Mall Customer Segmentation / Credit Card transactions) → Unsupervised learning: K-Means/DBSCAN clustering + PCA dimensionality reduction + Isolation Forest anomaly detection
description: The project applies unsupervised machine learning to discover hidden structures in data or detect outliers. The core mechanism uses K-Means or DBSCAN for customer segmentation alongside PCA for dimensionality reduction and visualization. The two scenarios compare identifying user personas via clustering versus using Isolation Forests to detect anomalous entries.
consultant_notes: Unsupervised methods are feasible but misaligned with the predictive pipeline brief.
annotator_notes: The methods are feasible and useful, but this plan does not fit the brief as well because the assignment asks for predictive classification or regression models with baseline comparison and evaluation on unseen data, while clustering/anomaly detection is mainly unsupervised and harder to evaluate clearly in that framework.
